cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law — Mary Russell married her brother Matthew Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: She and brother Matthew both married Hooker descendants
narrative_blocks.body: She married Captain Nathaniel Hooker — a direct Hooker-surname descendant. Her brother Matthew Talcott simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont line. Two of Governor Joseph Talcott's children marrying into the Hooker family in the same generation is why "Talcott" became a permanent name in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law — Mary Russell married her brother Matthew Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: She and brother Matthew both married Hooker descendants
narrative_blocks.body: She married Captain Nathaniel Hooker — a direct Hooker-surname descendant. Her brother Matthew Talcott simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont line. Two of Governor Joseph Talcott's children marrying into the Hooker family in the same generation is why "Talcott" became a permanent name in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Both families joined through Talcott siblings.
His wife's brother Matthew also married into the Hooker family line.
Both tied to separate Talcott branches in the same family generation.
Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Governor Talcott's daughter Eunice
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Talcott was the second daughter of Joseph Talcott, Governor of Connecticut from 1725 to 1741. Their marriage directly joined the two founding Hartford families — reinforced when Eunice's brother Matthew simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Named co-heir in the Governor's estate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 25, 1742, Nathaniel and Eunice appeared before the Court of Probate in Hartford to sign Governor Joseph Talcott's estate distribution as co-heirs. It was one of the final official acts binding the Hooker family to the colonial governor's wealth.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale man, militia captain, Assembly representative
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1729 and held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut Militia as early as 1734. He served as Hartford's representative in the General Assembly across three sessions, while running a substantial Hartford mercantile estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James partnered in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: His son James Hooker was a partner in the mercantile firm Hooker & Chaffee, which grew very successful during the Revolutionary War era. The firm continued the family's Hartford commercial standing into the next generation.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut. Cemetery linkage repair: existing person was listed in cemetery hooker_connections in prior pilots but burial.cemetery_id was null. Pilot104 closure of bidirectional reference.

Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut. Cemetery linkage repair: existing person was listed in cemetery hooker_connections in prior pilots but burial.cemetery_id was null. Pilot104 closure of bidirectional reference.

narrative_blocks.header: Eunice died young
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice died in 1760, aged about three. Eunice’s parents gave the name to a child born two years later.

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Cemetery, New London, New London County, Connecticut.

sources.short: Hart Family Genealogy, Hart Book entry #1765

cross_connections.display_label: His mother — daughter of Governor Joseph Talcott.
His maternal grandfather, Governor of Connecticut 1724-1741.
First cousin once removed via the Talcott family.
Wife of his cousin Wadsworth — also a Hooker descendant.
Father-in-law and partner in the Hooker and Chaffee firm.
his partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee
brother of his wife Mary Chaffee

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker and Chaffee supplied the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker and Chaffee was one of New England's largest mercantile houses before the Revolution, transacting much business for the Colonial Government. The partners gave freely to the patriot cause and were seriously embarrassed at war's end.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm provisioned alongside Wadsworth's commissary
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker and Chaffee firm operated alongside Jeremiah Wadsworth's larger commissary operation — both Hooker-Talcott descendants provisioning the same Continental Army. The Connecticut River Valley supply network was effectively a Hooker-tree internal collaboration.

narrative_blocks.header: His Windsor house is on the National Register
narrative_blocks.body: He built the James Hooker House ca. 1772 at 118 Palisado Avenue, Windsor — now part of the Palisado Avenue Historic District on the National Register of Historic Places. The interior was later gutted for Chaffee School classrooms but the Federal-period exterior survives under Greek Revival additions.

institutions.institution_blurb: Silent partner; one of New England's great Revolutionary-era trading houses.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this house c.1772; it survives on Palisado Avenue's National Register district.

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Captain James Hooker (1742-1805), Windsor CT merchant.

career.notes: Principal of INST070 Hooker and Chaffee. Succeeded to father's Hartford business with brothers; established Windsor branch. See INST070 for full firm history.

parents.mother_research_notes: Lydia (Griswold) Chaffee, b.Dec 17, 1722 Simsbury CT; d.Oct 1, 1801 Windsor CT. Daughter of the Griswold family of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother John was partner in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Her older brother John Chaffee was a partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee — the business linking her birth family directly to her husband's. The 1784 marriage to Captain James Hooker thus joined both personal and commercial lines of the Windsor Connecticut Chaffee household.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived 41 years past husband's 1805 death
narrative_blocks.body: She survived her husband Captain James Hooker by forty-one years — he died December 1805, she lived until October 1846 at eighty-six. After James's death she returned from Rochester New York to Windsor Connecticut and lived with her unmarried sister Hepsibah Chaffee until Hepsibah's 1834 death.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

cross_connections.display_label: His sisters' graves share her husband's burial plot at Vale

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker descendant; wife of Rev. Andrew Yates

burial.plot_notes: Vale Cemetery Schenectady NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Husband's ward became consort of Hawaii's last queen
narrative_blocks.body: The Dominis family — Captain John Dominis, wife Mary Jones, and their young son John Owen — boarded at 26 Front Street, the Yates home in Schenectady; John Owen was born there on March 10, 1832, and the Dominis daughters who died young were buried in the Yates family plot at Vale Cemetery alongside Hannah and her husband. That same boy went on to marry Queen Liliʻuokalani, the last sovereign monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi, becoming the kingdom's last and only male consort.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker women who married Strongs in 1729 and 1813

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband was Monroe County's first judge
narrative_blocks.body: Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong, who in 1821 became the first judge of Monroe County, New York. She died in Rochester in 1850; he remarried and moved to Detroit, dying there in 1867.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Hooker-Strong marriage echoed one from 1729
narrative_blocks.body: When Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813, she entered a family that had already wed into the Hooker line 84 years before: Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married her distant cousin Sarah Hooker at Farmington in 1729, and a grandson of that marriage — Asahel Hooker Strong — was already carrying her maiden name as his own middle name by 1776. Dolly's marriage completed the second arc; her great-grandson Beebe Hooker Strong, born in Milwaukee in 1866, would carry the name to a third generation on the Wisconsin frontier.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Alexander Hooker Papers, D.344 — University of Rochester Special Collections

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his dead half-brother Alexander Allin Hooker (b.1763; d.1781; FAG 22532503), son of Capt. James Hooker's second wife Hannah Allin — a memorial naming that also honors the Allin family name carried through Capt. James's second wife.

military_service.unit: Unknown — service documented in son's obituary
military_service.notes: Fought at Battle of Lundy's Lane (July 25, 1814) and Battle of Queenston Heights (Oct 13, 1812). Documented in obituary of his son Capt. James Henry Hooker, Rochester newspaper, March 1907: 'His father, Alexander B. Hooker, served in the war of 1812, fighting in the battles of Lundy's Lane, Queenstown, and others.'

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery — land he deeded to Irondequoit in 1840; family plot survived 1950s accidental destruction

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneer settler who gave Irondequoit its Hooker Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Alexander Allin Hooker arrived in the Rochester area in 1816 — with his brother Horace — to visit their sister Dolly (Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, who had recently married and settled in Canandaigua) and stayed to open a general store that lasted three years; by 1828 he had purchased Jesse Tainter's farm and by 1839 he was Irondequoit's first Town Clerk, and in 1840 he deeded land that became Hooker Cemetery to the town. His family burial plot there is one of the few in the cemetery to survive intact when the town accidentally destroyed most of the grounds in the 1950s, leaving his stone among the more legible remnants of the family that gave Irondequoit its first civic institutions.

narrative_blocks.header: Fought at Lundy's Lane before founding Irondequoit
narrative_blocks.body: Before settling in the Rochester area, Alexander Allin Hooker served in the War of 1812, fighting at the battles of Lundy's Lane (July 25, 1814, one of the bloodiest engagements on the Canadian border) and Queenston Heights — service documented in his son's 1907 newspaper obituary, which called him 'a soldier' who 'served in the war of 1812, fighting in the battles of Lundy's Lane, Queenstown, and others.' He came to Rochester in 1816, the year after the war ended, and within two decades had deeded a cemetery and founded a town — a veteran who became a civic patriarch.

narrative_blocks.header: An old Indian trail passed behind his cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: The burying ground Alexander Hooker made public in 1840 sat on a sandy knoll traversed by an Old Indian Trail that ran from the Seneca Village on Boughton Hill (near Victor, New York) to the Indian Landing at the mouth of the Genesee River — meaning the Hooker Cemetery was not merely a settler burial ground but sat astride an ancient Seneca thoroughfare, and likely contains Seneca burials alongside those of the earliest European settlers. It is estimated that perhaps a thousand people are buried in the one-and-a-half acres, most without markers, interred by pioneers under pine and oak trees before the ground was formally enclosed.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

notable.notable_blurb: Yale-trained lawyer; surrogate, presidential elector, canal commissioner

cross_connections.display_label: Yale 1810 classmates and first cousins on the Chaffee side
their wives, Catherine and Helen Reade, were sisters

bio.title: Judge
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of James Hooker (1792-1858), Surrogate of Dutchess County NY.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College Class of 1810. One of many Hooker-line Yale graduates spanning generations.
Judge — already wired in earlier stage.

education.notes: Admitted to bar in Poughkeepsie 1813 after Litchfield.

career.notes: Admitted to the bar in Poughkeepsie 1813.

career.organization: New York State Erie Canal Commission
career.notes: Appointed Canal Commissioner of New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter's line became Gilded Age Manhattan royalty
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker's daughter Catherine Livingston Hooker married Colonel John William Hamersley in 1840 — a Manhattan lawyer, traveler, and social figure who had met Muhammad Ali Pasha in Egypt and helped save the Mexican Republic from Napoleon — and their line grew into one of Gilded Age New York's most prominent families: the Colonel's son J. Hooker Hamersley published poetry, once courted Edith Roosevelt before she chose Theodore, and whose son Louis Gordon Hamersley was born to international headlines as the 'seven million dollar baby' in 1892. The Hooker who had been a Windsor merchant's son and Poughkeepsie probate lawyer thus became, within three generations, the progenitor of a Fifth Avenue dynasty that hosted a President's Cup speedboat ceremony and twelve hundred crippled veterans on a Hudson River estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve years as Surrogate of Dutchess County
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Surrogate of Dutchess County for twelve years from 1828 to 1840 — the probate judge responsible for wills, estates, and guardianships across one of New York's most prosperous Hudson Valley counties — and was selected as Presidential Elector of New York in 1836, before being appointed Canal Commissioner of New York in 1842, overseeing operations of the Erie Canal that had transformed the American interior. He had come to Poughkeepsie in 1813 after graduating Yale (1810) and studying at Litchfield Law School, the first law school in the United States, and spent his entire career in Dutchess County, dying there in September 1858.

narrative_blocks.header: Vestryman of Christ Church for forty-one years
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Vestryman of Christ Church in Poughkeepsie from 1817 — the year after he married Helen Sarah Reade and settled in Dutchess County — until his death in 1858, forty-one consecutive years in the same Episcopal congregation through the entire arc of his career as Surrogate, Canal Commissioner, and retired counsellor. This was a quiet denominational shift: the Hookers were descendants of the Puritan Rev. Thomas Hooker, a Congregationalist founder of Hartford, yet James spent his adult life in the Episcopal church — a shift that would be echoed when his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley was eulogized at Grace Church on Fifth Avenue, the grandest Episcopal church in Manhattan.

narrative_blocks.header: He married into the Reade Street Livingstons
narrative_blocks.body: On January 24, 1816, James Hooker married Helen Sarah Reade in Poughkeepsie — the granddaughter of John Reade (1745-1808) and Catherine Livingston Reade, connecting the Windsor Hooker family to both the Livingston Manor dynasty and to the colonial New York merchant family whose ancestor Joseph Reade had served as warden of Trinity Church for fifty years, sat on the Governor's Provincial Council, and given Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name. The marriage brought into the Hooker line a thread of colonial New York aristocracy that would eventually surface in his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley — a man who traced his ancestry to both Robert Livingston the Elder (grantee of Livingston Manor by royal charter) and the Reade family whose name still runs through Lower Manhattan.

parents.father_research_notes: James Hooker of Windsor CT — third wife Mary (Chaffee) Hooker.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary (Chaffee) Hooker (third wife of James Hooker)

notable.notable_blurb: Pioneer merchant; grandfather of Elon Hooker

careers.location: Bristol near Canandaigua NY → Carthage near Rochester NY
careers.notes: Per EH 798: 'He had the spirit of the pioneer and the glowing accounts of the Genesee Country attracted him to Western New York.' First lived briefly at Bristol near Canandaigua NY in mercantile business with brother Alexander Allin Hooker. Later moved to Carthage near Rochester to cooperate with brother-in-law Judge Strong (likely Elisha Beebe Strong 1788-1867, husband of Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong) in developing 'what was supposed to be the future city of Genesee.' For 20 years engaged in active commercial enterprises.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Rochester Commercial Nursery in 1844
narrative_blocks.body: He co-founded the Rochester Commercial Nursery in 1844, part of the wave of New England transplants who built Rochester into the nursery and seed capital of America. His son Horace B. studied under Colonel Bissell, then joined the nursery before becoming an engineer and contractor.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson Elon founded Hooker Chemical Company
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson Elon Huntington Hooker founded the Hooker Electrochemical Company at Niagara Falls in 1909, pioneering American production of chlorine and caustic soda. Three generations of Rochester Hookers carried the entrepreneurial spirit Edward Hooker described as 'the spirit of the pioneer.'

career.organization: Mercantile partnership with brother Alexander Allin Hooker, Bristol NY
career.notes: First settled at Bristol near Canandaigua NY as a merchant after migrating from Windsor CT in 1821.

career.organization: Carthage NY (with brother-in-law Judge Elisha Beebe Strong)
career.notes: Moved to Carthage near Rochester to cooperate with brother-in-law in developing 'what was supposed to be the future city of Genesee.' Engaged in active commercial enterprises for about twenty years.
Co-founded with partners in 1844 — part of Rochester's emergence as the 'Flower City.' Confirmed by University of Rochester Special Collections (Hooker Family Papers, D.130, 1790-1963, 27 boxes).

sources.short: Chaffee genealogy book, entry 702 (via mother Mary Chaffee)

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Mary Chaffee' directly for her mother Mary (Chaffee) Hooker (X00319), who was herself named for the Chaffee family.

narrative_blocks.header: Named directly for her mother Mary Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Chaffee Hooker was named in full for her mother Mary Chaffee Hooker (X00319), making her name a direct echo of the generation before her. She married Dr. James Magoffin of Schenectady and died there on March 20, 1839 at age forty-two, nearly a decade before her husband.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-two; six children survived her
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Chaffee Hooker Magoffin died on March 27, 1839 in Schenectady at age forty-two, leaving behind Doctor James C. Magoffin and six children, three of whom carried her maiden name 'Hooker' as their middle name — a deliberate act of genealogical memory that would persist through four more generations and eventually surface in the name of the 55th Secretary of the Treasury. She traveled from Windsor, Connecticut to Schenectady and died before seeing any grandchild; but the 'Hooker' she left in her children's names traveled farther than she did.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery Schenectady per FA
Oakwood Cemetery, Syracuse, Onondaga County, New York.

bio.photo_notes: Henry Thomas Hooker (1803-1893), Rochester merchant and Syracuse salt manufacturer

career.notes: One of the early merchants of Rochester NY.
Contributed to Syracuse's salt industry through ingenuity and practical mechanical skill. Syracuse was known as the 'Salt City' of nineteenth-century America.

narrative_blocks.header: Early merchant of Rochester; salt manufacturer in Syracuse
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Thomas Hooker was one of the early merchants of Rochester, New York, before moving to Syracuse where he became a salt manufacturer — contributing, per his obituary, 'not a little in its earlier days' through 'his ingenuity and practical mechanical skill' to a salt industry that made Syracuse the Salt City of nineteenth-century America, one of the nation's most important inland commercial centers. A 'thorough gentleman of the old school,' athlete in his youth, and man of 'strictest integrity, honorable and honored in his dealings,' he was a member of the Presbyterian church whose 'habits of strict temperance' aided 'a vigorous and hearty old age' — so that at ninety, 'his erect form and firm step gave him the appearance of a much younger man.'

narrative_blocks.header: His ingenuity helped build the Syracuse salt industry
narrative_blocks.body: The salt industry that made Syracuse the 'Salt City' of nineteenth-century America benefited specifically from Henry Thomas Hooker's ingenuity and practical mechanical skill — his obituary credits him with contributions to salt manufacturing 'not a little in its earlier days,' when the Onondaga salt springs were being developed into an industrial complex producing much of the nation's table salt and helping finance the Erie Canal at a moment when inland transportation was remaking American commerce. He was also, by all accounts, a man who 'never wearied in his reminiscences of his early sports and adventures' — a former athlete who attributed his longevity to strict temperance habits, the obituary noting that even at the end of his life 'his erect form and firm step gave him the appearance of a much younger man,' as if the athlete were still in there somewhere.

death.cause: Died Aug 27, 1763 — seven months after her father Nathaniel died Jan 27, 1763.

narrative_blocks.header: The firm's failure drove him from Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: As a partner in Hooker and Chaffee, he helped provision the Continental Army before French and Spanish spoliations brought the firm down after the war. He removed to Sackett's Harbor, New York, and later died at Herkimer; his wife Elizabeth survived him by just six months.

institutions.institution_blurb: Partner in the firm; dispersed to western New York after its failure.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-seven; two young children survived her
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Hooker married Richard Lord Jones in Hartford in 1793 and died on May 18, 1800 in Granville, New York, at twenty-seven — leaving behind two children, Elizabeth (born August 1792) and Horace (born January 1795), who were seven and five years old when she died. Her husband Richard Lord Jones outlived her by fifty-two years, dying in Indiana in 1852 at eighty-five; their daughter Elizabeth lived to eighty-nine, outliving her mother by eighty-two years.

burial.plot_notes: Old Yard, Middle Granville, Washington County NY per FA

political_offices.title: Governor
political_offices.notes: First Governor of Connecticut born within its limits. Longest-serving after Gov. Winthrop. Died in office Oct 11, 1741.

cross_connections.display_label: Son-in-law signed his estate — 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker'
His granddaughter Esther married Rev. Noadiah Russell in 1758
was the Deputy Governor whose death opened his path

notable.notable_blurb: First Connecticut governor born within its limits

narrative_blocks.header: First Connecticut governor born within the colony
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Hartford on November 16, 1669, Joseph Talcott was the first Governor of Connecticut born within the colony rather than in England. He served from 1724 until his death on October 11, 1741 — 17 years in office, longer than any predecessor except Governor Winthrop and any successor until the modern era. His funeral expenses of £69 12s. 6d. were paid from the Colonial Treasury.

narrative_blocks.header: He presided in the upper house the afternoon
narrative_blocks.body: When his wife Eunice died suddenly about noon on Thursday, May 25, 1738, the Deputy Governor was absent at Norwich. Governor Talcott did not let his private grief interfere with his official duty — he returned and presided in the upper house that same afternoon. The General Assembly appointed a committee to draw an address of condolence to the Governor.

narrative_blocks.header: His estate was signed by 'Nathaniel Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: At the Court of Probate, Hartford, on December 25, 1742, the distribution of Governor Talcott's estate was signed by all children as co-heirs. Among the signatures: 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker' — his daughter Eunice Talcott and her husband Nathaniel Hooker 2d. The Talcott-Hooker convergence was entered into the public record.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his children each found a separate
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Eunice married Nathaniel Hooker 2d — direct Hooker surname. His son Matthew married Mary Russell, a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont branch. His daughter Abigail married Rev. Daniel Wadsworth; their son Jeremiah later married Mary's own sister Mehitable Russell, also a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Petitioned for his father's estate by primogeniture
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Talcott was the fourth son of Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr. but the eldest to survive his father. In 1691, at age 21, he petitioned the General Court for his father's estate by right of primogeniture — an assertive legal claim for colonial Connecticut. He was first mentioned in public life in 1711 as a committee member to lay out the town of Coventry.

bio.notes: Widow of [first husband Wakeman] before marrying Gov. Joseph Talcott (T00045)

political_offices.title: Deputy / Assistant Magistrate
political_offices.notes: Elected before Connecticut joined New Haven Colony

political_offices.title: Treasurer
political_offices.notes: Succeeded his father May 17, 1660; held office until 1676

political_offices.title: Major, then Lieut.-Colonel
political_offices.notes: Resigned Treasurer and took command of Connecticut army at outbreak of King Philip's War

notable.notable_blurb: Colonial military commander, King Philip's War

narrative_blocks.header: He was named guardian of the charter
narrative_blocks.body: When Connecticut's royal charter arrived from Charles the Second in 1662, the freemen chose Talcott, with Samuel Wyllys and Lieutenant John Allyn, as the committee sworn to keep it in their custody. A quarter-century later he would help spirit that same charter away from Andros.

narrative_blocks.header: He took the charter as the candles died
narrative_blocks.body: By Roger Wolcott's 1764 account, when the candles were snuffed in the Hartford meeting-house in 1687, Talcott and Nathaniel Stanley lifted the two colonial charters from the table before Andros could seize them. Wolcott's telling names no oak; the tree entered the tradition later, and the duplicate passed to his son-in-law Joseph Wadsworth.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut's victorious commander in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott led Connecticut's forces through King Philip's War in 1675 and 1676, winning a string of engagements that earned him promotion to lieutenant-colonel. His campaigns ranged from the river towns down into the Narragansett country.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the colony's purse for sixteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott served as treasurer of Connecticut Colony from 1660 to 1676, taking the office directly from his father before him. The family's account books became a running ledger of early Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: He died intestate, his estate worth thousands
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott died in 1688 without a will, leaving an estate appraised at more than two thousand pounds, a substantial colonial fortune. It passed by primogeniture to his eldest surviving son, the future governor Joseph Talcott.

battles: Multiple engagements with Wampanoag and allied tribes
military_service.notes: Led Connecticut standing army with 200 Mohegan and Pequot Indian allies. 'In the various battles with the Indians in which he was engaged, he was always victorious, and obtained great renown as an Indian fighter.' Promoted from Major to Lieut.-Colonel during the war.

parents.notes: Her father John Wakeman was Colony Treasurer — the same office her husband John Talcott Jr. would later hold
parents.father_research_notes: John Wakeman, Treasurer of the Colony of Connecticut

burialCemetery.name: Palisado Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Colonial-era cemetery on Palisado Avenue, Windsor CT. Burial place of Eunice Talcott Hooker (I00054), daughter of Governor Joseph Talcott, and James Hooker (H00348). Per Henry Reed Stiles, Stiles Family in America (1895), Job Stiles (H00462) and Mary Drake Stiles (I00624) were buried 'on one stone' here in what the 1895 book called the 'Old Windsor Graveyard' — the Palisado is the formal modern name.

crossConnections.display_label: Sister-in-law — Mary Russell married her brother Matthew Talcott.
